```
**Offer Creation Cheat Sheet**  
**([Original training doc here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1YDF2WPKD3JHSDEF-PGkXx26_gYNYQmknJFIW-VzSjQA/edit))**

**Niche (3 S’s)**

* Specific Person  
  * Non-technical professionals and solo entrepreneurs who already want to build and launch an AI-powered app around their domain expertise.  
* Specific Problem  
  * They can’t turn a clear app idea into a working, deployed product without a technical cofounder. They stall on tooling, workflow, debugging, and deployment; lose momentum; and never ship.  
* Specific Way (Method / Process / *Unique Mechanism*)  
  * The AI Co‑Founder Method — a step-by-step AI-native build workflow that replaces the need for a technical cofounder by pairing domain expertise with an integrated toolchain (Cursor \+ Claude Code \+ Lovable \+ GitHub \+ Vercel) and build scripts, so non-technical pros go from validated concept to deployed app in 90 days and can fix, extend, and own what they build.

**High Level Promise**

* **Outcome:** Ship a working, deployed AI app built around your expertise, with first real users and a clear iteration roadmap / no CTO, no cofounder, no equity lost.  
* **Container (Timeline):** 90 days.  
* **Guarantee:** Launch a published ai powered app within 90 days or you don't pay

**Pillars**  
1- Blueprint to Backlog: Lock the “smallest shippable” app by converting your idea into a lean spec, user stories, and prompts; validate scope with 1–3 real users for quick wins.  
2 \- Build with AI Pairing: Execute focused sprints inside Cursor \+ Claude Code \+ Lovable; use GitHub for safe versioning so features generate, refactor, and wire up fast.  
3- Ship & Deploy: One-click Vercel deploy, env keys, basic CLI hygiene, and a first-users handoff so someone outside your bubble can use it today.  
4 \- Own & Extend: Add lightweight auth/security, run a bug bash, and learn to debug, patch, and integrate APIs while maintaining a living backlog so you can keep shipping without a cofounder.

**Fulfillment/Delivery**  
Skool based async curriculum mapped to the 4 pillars with concise “watch-and-do” modules and copy-paste assets (prompts, component templates, deploy checklists). 2–3 live group calls per week for hot seats, build reviews, and unblock sessions. 1:1 kickoff to define your concept, 60‑day build path, and checkpoint plan. Dedicated community space for daily questions; 24–48 hr feedback on code snippets, errors, and deploy blockers. Weekly “Ship Friday” ritual with a public demo template to drive accountability and first-user velocity.

**Pricing**  
$7,500 Group Program With Modules In Skool \+ Group Calls   
$12,000 Group Program With Modules In Skool \+ Group Calls \+ 1 Call Bi Weekly \+ 6 hours

**Client Results (Please add 3+ case studies if you have them)**

1. Ava went from first-time vibecoder to launching her own tech podcast, The Incognito Mode, in a single night, and was featured on CBS and ABC News within months, by attending one of Marko's vibecoding workshops  
     
2. Jawna is a neuroscientist who was stuck, overthinking, and hesitating on the technical edges of her product — shipped an actual working app for tracking her blood sugar in a single night at one of Marko's vibecoding workshops in SF.

3. Sophie, a 2x founder and ex-professional athlete, built and launched a retro arcade video game in 1.5 hours at a hackathon and won 3rd place.

   

   

           Create the CLAUDE or whatever we need within (72 hrs)

Finish the program (14 days)

Attend every single call for the entire call (3 months)

Launch at least 3 variations (HAVE TO)

For the 10% of people that fail….. Half of them will ask for the refund, half of them will want it
```

```
This worksheet is designed to help you develop the “pitching phase” of your Pitch Codex™.

This is the MOST important part. This phase (and worksheet) can also be used to create (or refine) your offer. 

Do not forget \- your offer/pitch is the MOST IMPORTANT PART of the entire call. Especially with how competition is today. 

Take your time with this, and let me know if you have questions / need feedback.    
---

**Step 1: Get Clear On The Transformation**

What is your clients current situation vs desired situation? List that below. 

**Current**: Non-technical founder who wants to build an app but feels blocked, thinks they need engineers or cofounder, intimidated by coding and AI. No clear starting point.

- I have an idea but no idea where to start  
- I feel like I need a technical cofounder  
- I open Cursor or Claude and feel overwhelmed  
- I don’t trust what AI gives me  
- I get stuck after the first step  
- I overthink instead of building

**Desired**: Independent builder who can go from idea → live app quickly using AI, no reliance on engineers, can debug, design, and ship repeated. Building apps feels routine, not intimidating. Can execute on any idea on demand.

- I can build and launch my own app without needing anyone  
- I understand what I built and can fix it or improve it myself  
- I’ve actually put something real in users’ hands  
- I have early users giving feedback and validating the idea  
- I feel like a builder, not just a person with ideas  
- I know how to go from idea → working product repeatedly

**Step 2:** **List All The Steps**

Think of all of the clients you’ve worked with in the past (or possibly even your own transformation) what are the steps you had to take to go from current → desired situation?   
List ALL of them in chronological order below:   
(Tip: Consider foundational roadblocks, tactical roadblocks, mindset issues, biggest fears, where your clients got stuck/needed most support in the past, etc)

**Summary**

1. Start with your first app: Write your idea in plain english in detail  
2. Open the AI coding editor and build your real app immediately (send immediately to Marko)  
3. Learn how to work with the AI to solve problems (break things down into steps, diagnose issues, enforce standards) (Unstuck System)  
4. Learn the framework for reliable vibecoding (5 step process)  
5. Get into a launching rhythm (idea → build → debug → deploy)  
6. Repeat 30x: Launch 30 apps in 30 days publicly (drill the process into your brain) Build and ship solutions consistently by turning real problems into working apps each week  
   1. Remove fear \+ gain speed through repetition   
7. Learn how to become independent (not reliant on instructor to answer your questions)  
   1. AKA learn how to use AI to debug your problems and teach you new concepts  
8. Learn how to generate lots of ideas  
9. Revisit your business idea now with a higher technical understanding

   

**Step 3: Get Clear On Which Steps Are Most Essential**

Answer the following questions to help you get more clarity on your steps:

* **Are there any steps that will give your clients a quick win?** 

Primary quick win \= actually building their own app within 48 hours

- The first 2 steps basically


* **Are there any steps that will give your clients an immediate sense of clarity?** 

Yes, probably writing out your idea on paper. Also seeing version 1 of their app and realizing it’s both not as complicated as they thought but also where they might want to revise some assumptions they previously had about their idea. Or even now start to think about the branding of the app but clarity comes from doing, not explanation

* **What are the 20% of the steps that drive 80% of the results?** 

Write your idea in plain english in detail, use meta-prompting to \*enhance\* it (optional), build it quickly, then use the 5-Step Vibecoding Framework, and building a ton of apps, and posting them publicly. Repetition (30 apps) is the primary driver of transformation. 

After this, it would be getting into the pattern of identifying the 1-2 features in your app that are driving 80% of the interest and removing all the unnecessary features so these are the only things the user can do on your app.

* **What are the 20% of the steps most desired by 80% of the market?** 

People want to turn their idea into a working app quickly, ideally in one or a few prompts and have something that looks good, works immediately, and has security built-in. 

Reality: that outcome requires iteration and repetition. But what I can do here is I have a secret prompt that I frequently use in my personal vibe-coding to get great results off-the-bat that I can share with clients that will *enhance* their project idea
```

```
**PRE-PITCH / TRANSITION**

Can I give you some thoughts? The reason you're stuck on your app idea is because you're thinking like someone who needs permission to build, instead of someone who just builds.

And that's a problem because while you're researching frameworks and looking for technical cofounders, other people with worse ideas but better execution are already launching and getting users. You're stuck in planning mode while opportunities pass you by.

Does that make sense?

So if you're serious about turning your ideas into real apps that people actually use, what you're going to have to do \- whether you work with me or figure it out yourself \- is stop thinking like someone who can't build and start operating like someone who can solve any problem with AI on demand.

But do you see how if you had those abilities, you could easily turn any business idea into a working product without waiting on anyone else?

Do you want me to show you exactly how we help you make that transformation?

**PILLAR 1: THE 48-HOUR LAUNCH™**

Listen, if you're serious about finally turning your ideas into working apps, the first thing we have to do is prove to ourselves that this is actually possible.

You know how you mentioned feeling stuck on your idea and like you'd need an engineer or technical cofounder to actually build it? And that's been frustrating because you have this vision but no way to make it real?

Here's what happens to most people \- they get excited about an app idea, maybe they even write it down or sketch it out. But then they hit that wall where they think "okay, now I need to find a developer" or "I need to learn to code" and the whole thing just... stops. Sound familiar?

So what we do first is take that exact idea you've been carrying around and turn it into a real, working web app within 48 hours of starting the program.

I'm talking about something with your actual features \- maybe it's a simple booking system, a basic marketplace, or a tool that solves that specific problem you identified. It'll have a clean interface, working buttons, maybe a simple database to store information. Nothing fancy, but it works.

You can share the link with anyone, they can actually use it, and you can start getting real feedback. Think of it like a working prototype that proves your concept actually makes sense.

We give you our exact templates, the specific AI prompts we use, and we literally walk you through each step \- from writing out your idea clearly, to getting it deployed with a real web address that anyone can visit.

And here's why this matters \- once you see people actually clicking around in something you built, everything changes. You go from "I wish I could build this" to "okay, now I know this works \- what should I add next?"

You know what I mean?

What questions do you have about that part?

Do you feel like having your actual app live and working within 48 hours would help you \[achieve their goal\]?

**PILLAR 2: THE BREAKTHROUGH ACCELERATOR™**

Perfect. So now you have your app live and working, which is huge. But here's what happens next \- you're going to want to add features, fix bugs, make improvements. And the moment something doesn't work exactly how you expected, most people freeze up.

You know how you said you've tried AI tools before but got frustrated when they didn't give you exactly what you wanted? And that made you think "maybe I'm just not technical enough for this"?

Here's the thing \- most people treat AI like a vending machine. They put in a request and expect perfect output. But when something breaks or looks wrong, they don't know how to guide the AI to fix it. So they get stuck for hours or just give up completely.

But the real problem isn't that you're not technical enough \- it's that you don't know how to communicate with AI yet. Most people try to build their whole app in one shot, get something that's 70% right, and then don't know what to do next.

So what we do is teach you our Breakthrough Accelerator™. We have a saying in the program: "Your complaint is your prompt." Instead of getting frustrated, you learn to turn any problem into clear instructions that AI can actually solve.

We show you our Diagnostic Framework \- how to break down any issue into simple steps. Our Error Translation Guide that turns scary technical messages into plain English problems. And most importantly, how to collaborate with AI instead of just hoping it reads your mind.

This isn't about becoming a programmer. This is about becoming someone who can direct AI to build what you actually want, one piece at a time.

You know what I mean?

What questions do you have about that part?

Do you feel like knowing how to get unstuck quickly would help you \[build apps confidently without getting blocked\]?
```

```
    // Share functionality
    function shareToSlack() {
```

```
h1 {

.container {

section {

    transform: translateY(20px);

}

section:nth-child(2) { animation-delay: 0.1s; }
section:nth-child(3) { animation-delay: 0.2s; }
section:nth-child(4) { animation-delay: 0.3s; }

@keyframes fadeInUp {

}

.hero {

@keyframes pulse {

}

}
```

```
    // Update capacity information
    function updateSeatsLeft() {
        const seatsElement = document.querySelector('.seats-left');
        // if (seatsElement) {
        //     // Keep it static and professional

    // Initialize on load
    document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
        // updateSocialTicker();
        addUrgencyCountdown();
        updateSeatsLeft();
        hydrateEmailFromStorage();
        attachReserveListeners();
        
        // Update seats counter every hour
        setInterval(updateSeatsLeft, 60 * 60 * 1000);
    });

    function attachReserveListeners() {
        const inputs = document.querySelectorAll('.hero-input, .cta-input');
        inputs.forEach(input => {
            input.addEventListener('input', () => {
                input.classList.remove('input-error');
            });
        });
    }
```

# Context from my IDE setup:

## Open tabs:
- reserve.js: api/reserve.js

## My request for Codex:
How would you rate this website out of ten?What's the perfect way to even ask this?This is really not meant to do cold selling itself, but really for people who I'm talking to in person who asked to see a website.


---

_**Agent (gpt-5.5 2026-05-03 21:19:34Z)**_

For the job you described, I’d rate it around **7/10**.

Not as a cold-traffic sales page. As a **credibility page for warm leads who already met you**, it’s much closer. It explains the offer, shows there is a real container, gives a price, includes social proof, and makes you look like there is an actual program behind the conversation.

The biggest thing holding it back is that it still feels a little like a converted workshop page. It communicates “this is real,” but not yet “this is the obvious next step after talking to Marko.” For warm leads, the site should mainly do three things:

1. Confirm they understood the offer correctly.
2. Make the program feel serious and structured.
3. Remove doubt that this is just a casual vibe-coding class.

The perfect way to ask this would be something like:

> “Review this website as a warm-lead credibility page, not a cold sales page. Assume the visitor has already spoken with me in person, understands the basic offer, and wants to verify that the program is real, structured, and worth considering. Rate it out of 10 for that purpose, identify what creates confidence, what creates doubt, and suggest only minimal copy changes that would make it feel more aligned with a high-ticket 8-week program.”

Or even sharper:

> “Does this page support an in-person sales conversation, or does it accidentally weaken it?”

That’s the right frame. You’re not asking “is this the best landing page?” You’re asking: **after someone meets me, does this page increase trust or create friction?**

My quick read: it increases trust overall, but it could still be more precise about the program depth, who it’s for, and why the price makes sense.

_**Agent (gpt-5.5 2026-05-03 21:21:12Z)**_

Yes. Answer these in whatever format is easiest. Short bullets are fine.

**Audience**
1. Who is the exact person most likely to ask for the website after meeting you?
2. Are they usually founders, operators, creators, professionals with domain expertise, students, or something else?
3. Are they already sold on AI coding, curious but skeptical, or intimidated?
4. Do they usually already have an app idea, or are they more generally interested in learning to build?
5. What income/business stage are they typically at where `$7,500` makes sense?

**Context**
6. Where are these conversations usually happening: events, workshops, dinners, coworking spaces, DMs, referrals?
7. What have you usually told them before they visit the page?
8. What question do they ask right before you send the site?
9. What do you want them thinking 30 seconds after opening the site?
10. What would make them say, “Oh, this is more serious than I thought”?

**Offer**
11. Is the real program definitely 8 weeks, or is it closer to 90 days?
12. Is the primary promise one launched app, many apps, or becoming the kind of person who can build apps repeatedly?
13. What is the most accurate outcome: “published AI app,” “MVP with users,” “30 apps,” “independent builder,” or something else?
14. Do you want to emphasize the first app in 48 hours as the hook?
15. Is the 48-hour app usually their main business idea, a smaller first version, or a confidence-building app?
16. How many apps do participants actually build during the program?
17. What must they do to qualify for the guarantee, if anything?
18. Do you want the guarantee on the page, or only in conversation?

**Mechanism**
19. What do you actually call the program: Vibecoding Course, AI Co-Founder Method, Cursor & Beyond, or something else?
20. Which mechanism name feels most real to you: AI Co-Founder Method, 48-Hour Launch, Unstuck System, Builder’s Mastery Engine, Market Validation Accelerator?
21. Are those names meant to be public-facing, or are they more internal sales-call language?
22. What is the real “secret sauce” people should understand from the site?
23. Is this mostly about tools, mindset, repetition, technical independence, or market validation?
24. What do students get from you specifically that they would not get from a video course or YouTube?

**Delivery**
25. Is it Skool-based?
26. Are there async modules?
27. How many live calls per week are real and sustainable?
28. Are there 1:1 calls included at `$7,500`?
29. Is the 1:1 kickoff included?
30. Is there daily community support?
31. What is the expected response time for help?
32. Are calls Zoom, in-person, hybrid, or flexible?
33. Do people need to attend every call?
34. What happens if they miss calls?
35. Is the cohort capped at 5, or is that old workshop scarcity?

**Positioning**
36. Is this for people who “don’t code,” or people who are willing to become technical enough to own their product?
37. Do you want to say “no technical cofounder needed” directly?
38. Do you want to position this against hiring developers?
39. Do you want to position this against learning to code traditionally?
40. Do you want to position this against Lovable/Bolt-style prototype tools?
41. Should the page mention Cursor heavily, or should Cursor be one tool inside a broader AI build workflow?
42. Is “vibecoding” still the right word for this offer, or does it lower perceived seriousness?

**Trust**
43. Which testimonials are strongest and most relevant to this 8-week offer?
44. Do any current testimonials accidentally make it feel like a fun workshop instead of a serious program?
45. Do you have proof of people launching real apps, getting users, making money, getting press, or winning competitions?
46. Which case studies are you comfortable naming publicly?
47. Do you want the page to show your personal credibility more clearly?
48. What should someone believe about you after reading the page?

**Commitment**
49. How much work should participants expect per week?
50. Do you want to make the workload explicit?
51. Should the page screen out casual people?
52. What kind of person should not join?
53. Should “serious founders” be said directly, or is that too heavy-handed?
54. Is the tone supposed to feel premium, scrappy, founder-y, playful, or practical?

**CTA**
55. What is the actual next step: pay, apply, book a call, reserve a spot, DM you, or email?
56. Should someone be able to pay directly from the page?
57. Is direct checkout appropriate for a `$7,500` warm-lead offer?
58. Would “Apply for the next cohort” be better than “Reserve my spot”?
59. Do you want the page to help you qualify people before they buy?
60. What information do you need from them before accepting them?

**Page Experience**
61. What parts of the current page feel most “you”?
62. What parts feel inherited from the old workshop page?
63. What sections do you definitely want to keep recognizable?
64. What language currently makes you cringe or feel inaccurate?
65. What language do you strongly want to preserve?
66. When someone sees the page, should they feel excited, challenged, reassured, or impressed?
67. What is the one objection this page most needs to neutralize?
68. What is the one thing the page must not overpromise?

**Final Calibration**
69. If this page were perfect for warm leads, what would happen more often?
70. What would a bad version of this page accidentally make people think?
71. After someone reads it, what should they message you?
72. What do you already know you want changed?

```diff
+# Warm Lead Positioning Notes
+
+These notes capture the current intent for the landing page. The page is not meant to cold-sell strangers. It is meant to support an in-person conversation when someone asks to see a website.
+
+## Primary Visitor
+
+The most likely visitor is a warm lead who met Marko in person and asked for a website afterward.
+
+They are usually a non-technical person with ideas for apps or software they want to build, but they do not know how to turn those ideas into something real.
+
+Examples:
+
+- Working professionals
+- College students
+- Recruiters
+- Marketers
+- Nurses
+- Other domain experts with app ideas
+
+They are not necessarily startup CEOs or engineering leaders. They are more likely people with domain knowledge, curiosity, and ideas, but without the technical confidence to build.
+
+## Current Situation
+
+They have ideas for apps, automations, or software products, but they feel blocked.
+
+Common beliefs and fears:
+
+- Their idea feels too big.
+- They feel like they need an engineer.
+- They feel overwhelmed by the tools.
+- They do not know where to start.
+- They do not know how to break the idea down into something buildable.
+- They may have a business idea, but they do not know how to turn it into software.
+
+## Program Container
+
+The program is a two-month / 8-week program.
+
+Earlier docs may say 90 days, but the current direction is 8 weeks. The program changed when the "30 apps in 30 days" concept was replaced with building one app per week.
+
+The current program should be described as an 8-week build cycle, not a one-day workshop, a three-session sprint, or a 90-day program.
+
+## Weekly Build Rhythm
+
+The program is focused on weekly building across 8 weeks.
+
+The idea is not just to build one thing once. The deeper transformation is that participants build the skill of being able to build repeatedly.
+
+The weekly-build rhythm should support the promise that building software with AI becomes a repeatable skill, not a one-off assisted project.
+
+## Price
+
+The current price is $7,500.
+
+However, the page may not need to show the price. Since this is a high-ticket program and the page is for warm leads, showing the price too early may create unnecessary friction before the value is fully understood.
+
+The page should likely move away from direct checkout language and toward a call-based next step.
+
+## CTA Direction
+
+The likely goal of the page is to book a call.
+
+Possible next steps:
+
+- Book a call
+- Fill out an application form
+- Pick a date for a conversation
+
+Direct checkout may not be ideal for a $7,500 warm-lead offer. The page should probably support the sales conversation rather than try to close the sale by itself.
+
+Open question: whether the best CTA is "Book a call" directly or "Apply for the next cohort" leading to a form.
+
+## Outcome
+
+The outcome is not "your app with paid users."
+
+Paid users are not part of the guarantee and should not be implied.
+
+The real outcome is closer to:
+
+- Build and launch real apps with AI.
+- Put software in front of real users.
+- Learn the repeatable skill of building apps independently.
+- Become someone who can turn ideas into working software without waiting on an engineer.
+
+The page should emphasize skill acquisition and repeated building, not a revenue guarantee.
+
+## Promise Boundaries
+
+The page should not promise:
+
+- Paid users
+- Revenue
+- A guaranteed business outcome
+- That the program replaces all engineering needs forever
+- That every participant will launch a polished company-ready product
+
+The page can promise:
+
+- A structured 8-week build process
+- A repeatable AI app-building workflow
+- Support getting unstuck
+- Building and shipping weekly
+- Increased technical confidence
+- Real software built around the participant's own ideas
+
+## Who This Is Not For
+
+This is not for CEOs buying training for their engineering teams.
+
+This is not primarily for businesses trying to automate internal sales, operations, or admin workflows.
+
+This is not an enterprise AI enablement program.
+
+This is not a general business automation consulting offer.
+
+## Who This Is For
+
+This is for non-engineers who want to build apps, automations, or software that could become businesses.
+
+They may be working professionals, students, or domain experts. The common thread is that they have ideas and want to become capable of building them without being dependent on an engineer from day one.
+
+## Page Strategy
+
+The page should make the program feel credible, structured, and serious after someone has already met Marko.
+
+It should not try to do heavy cold-sales persuasion.
+
+The page should answer:
+
+- Is this real?
+- Is this structured?
+- Is this for someone like me?
+- Will this help me stop feeling blocked?
+- What is the next step if I want to explore it?
+
+The page should avoid:
+
+- Workshop language
+- Direct checkout framing
+- Overpromising business outcomes
+- Positioning the program as corporate training
+- Over-indexing on teams, CTO replacement, or enterprise ROI
```
</details></tool-use>

```
## Outcome

The outcome is not "your app with paid users."

Paid users are not part of the guarantee and should not be implied.

The real outcome is closer to:

- Build and launch real apps with AI.
- Put software in front of real users.
- Learn the repeatable skill of building apps independently.
- Become someone who can turn ideas into working software without waiting on an engineer.

The page should emphasize skill acquisition and repeated building, not a revenue guarantee.

## Promise Boundaries

The page should not promise:

- Paid users
- Revenue
- A guaranteed business outcome
- That the program replaces all engineering needs forever
- That every participant will launch a polished company-ready product

The page can promise:

- A structured 8-week build process
- A repeatable AI app-building workflow
- Support getting unstuck
- Building and shipping weekly
- Increased technical confidence
- Real software built around the participant's own ideas

## Who This Is Not For

This is not for CEOs buying training for their engineering teams.

This is not primarily for businesses trying to automate internal sales, operations, or admin workflows.

This is not an enterprise AI enablement program.

This is not a general business automation consulting offer.

## Who This Is For

This is for non-engineers who want to build apps, automations, or software that could become businesses.

They may be working professionals, students, or domain experts. The common thread is that they have ideas and want to become capable of building them without being dependent on an engineer from day one.

## Page Strategy

The page should make the program feel credible, structured, and serious after someone has already met Marko.

It should not try to do heavy cold-sales persuasion.

The page should answer:

- Is this real?
- Is this structured?
- Is this for someone like me?
- Will this help me stop feeling blocked?
- What is the next step if I want to explore it?

The page should avoid:

- Workshop language
- Direct checkout framing
- Overpromising business outcomes
- Positioning the program a
... (truncated)
```
</details></tool-use>

My pick right now is:

It says who it’s for, what they want, and avoids sounding like corporate training.

**What Hormozi Would Say**
Hormozi would probably push on three things:

1. **The outcome is still a little fuzzy.**
   He’d ask: “What exactly do I get?” Not emotionally, literally. Is it one app? A workflow? confidence? support? lifetime access? calls? He’d want the offer stack to be concrete.

2. **The CTA should match the sales process.**
   If this is high ticket, he’d probably agree with removing checkout and price from the page. `Schedule a call` is clearer than `Reserve my spot` if the close happens live.

3. **The value equation needs more specificity.**
   He’d want to increase dream outcome and perceived likelihood, while reducing time delay and effort. So the page should emphasize:
   - your own app, not fake projects
   - weekly calls
   - unstuck support between calls
   - lifetime access
   - cutting-edge workflows
   - no staying blocked for days

He would probably dislike “cohort forming now” if there are no cohorts. It creates fake scarcity and confusion.

**On The Weekly Sprints**
Your clarification matters. I would not make “one app per week” a headline. That creates the wrong focal point.

Better framing:

> Each week includes a focused 24-hour build sprint so you practice the workflow repeatedly while continuing to move your core app forward.

That belongs in the experience/modules area, not the hero.

**Hero**
I agree my suggested hero copy was too wordy. I’d keep the hero simpler:

Badge:

> For non-engineers who want to build real apps

Headline:

> You have an app idea.  
> Now learn how to build it.

Lead:

> An 8-week program where you work on your own app, get weekly calls and between-call support, and learn the AI workflows to keep building without waiting on an engineer.

That is still a little long, but acceptable as a lead. The details can move below.

**Other Experts Worth Asking**
I’d ask different experts for different things:

- **Alex Hormozi**: offer clarity, value stack, CTA, perceived value.
- **April Dunford**: positioning. Who is this really for, and what category does it belong in?
- **Donald Miller / StoryBrand**: clarity. Can a warm lead understand the page in 10 seconds?
- **Joanna Wiebe / Copyhackers**: conversion copy, voice-of-customer, reducing friction.
- **Seth Godin**: trust, worldview, whether the page feels honest and specific.
- **Rob Fitzpatrick / The Mom Test**: whether the page overclaims or hides assumptions about demand.
- **Lenny Rachitsky-style product lens**: ICP, activation moment, what “success” actually means.

For this page, I’d prioritize **Hormozi + Dunford + StoryBrand**. Offer, positioning, clarity. That’s the triangle.

- what they want
- what support solves

```
    display: inline-block;
    padding: 0.5rem 1rem;
    background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.15);
    border-radius: 100px;
    font-size: 0.875rem;
    font-weight: 600;
    margin-bottom: 1rem;
}

.price-main {
    display: inline-flex;
    align-items: baseline;
    justify-content: center;
    gap: 0.75rem;
    margin-bottom: 0.75rem;
}

.price-original {
    font-size: 1.5rem;
    font-weight: 600;
    color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.6);
    text-decoration: line-through;
}

.price-current {
    font-size: 2.75rem;
    font-weight: 800;
    line-height: 1;
    background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(249, 115, 22, 0.95) 0%, rgba(107, 70, 193, 0.95) 100%);
    -webkit-background-clip: text;
    -webkit-text-fill-color: transparent;
    background-clip: text;
}

.price-period {
    font-size: 1rem;
    opacity: 0.9;
}
```

```
    /**
     * Handles the Schedule a Call flow:
     * - Prevents default form submit
     * - Validates email from hero or CTA inputs
     * - Gives inline feedback on input errors
     * - Saves valid email to localStorage
     * - Fires tracking event via gtag if available
     * - Shows feedback to user
     */
    
    async function reserveSpot(event) {
        console.log('[ReserveSpot] Function called');
        
        if (event) {
            event.preventDefault();
            console.log('[ReserveSpot] Event prevented');
        }

        const form = event?.target;
        const heroInput = document.querySelector('.hero-input');
        const ctaInput = document.querySelector('.cta-input');
        const sourceInput = form ? form.querySelector('input[type="email"]') : heroInput || ctaInput;
        const email = sourceInput ? sourceInput.value.trim() : '';
        
        console.log('[ReserveSpot] Email extracted:', email);

        if (!email || !email.includes('@')) {
            console.log('[ReserveSpot] Invalid email, showing error');
            if (sourceInput) {
                sourceInput.classList.add('input-error');
                sourceInput.focus();
            }
            return;
        }

        console.log('[ReserveSpot] Email valid, syncing inputs...');
        [heroInput, ctaInput].forEach(input => {
            if (input) {
                input.classList.remove('input-error');
                if (!input.value) {
                    input.value = email;
                }
            }
        });

        try {
            localStorage.setItem('vibecoding_pending_email', email);
            console.log('[ReserveSpot] Email saved to localStorage');
        } catch (err) {
            console.warn('[ReserveSpot] Email persistence skipped:', err);
        }

        if (typeof gtag === 'function') {
            gtag('event', 'reserve_spot', {
                'event_category': 'conversion',
                'event_label': 'schedule_call_click'
            });
            console.log('[ReserveSpot] Analytics event fired');
        }

        showReserveFeedback('Saving your email...');
        console.log('[ReserveSpot] Calling /api/reserve endpoint...');

        try {
            const API_BASE_URL = 'https://webhooks.shipmas.fun';

            const response = await fetch(`${API_BASE_URL}/api/reserve`, {
              method: 'POST',
              headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
              body: JSON.stringify({ email }),
              mode: 'cors'
            });
            console.log('[ReserveSpot] API response status:', response.status);

            if (!response.ok) {
                const errorText = await response.text();
                console.error('[ReserveSpot] API error response:', errorText);
                throw new Error(`Server responded with ${response.status}`);
            }

            const data = await response.json();
            console.log('[ReserveSpot] API success:', data);
            
            showReserveFeedback('Got it. We’ll follow up with the scheduling link.');
        } catch (error) {
            console.error('[ReserveSpot] Reservation email capture failed:', error);
            showReserveFeedback('We saved your email locally in case the network hiccups.');
        }
        console.log('[ReserveSpot] Complete');
    }
```

# Context from my IDE setup:

## Open tabs:
- warm_lead_positioning_notes.md: warm_lead_positioning_notes.md
- reserve.js: api/reserve.js

## My request for Codex:
I kind of want to replace the 48-hour launch with deploy it live.II think overall, I like the old experience better than the new experience, but I can't exactly put my finger on why.


---

_**Agent (gpt-5.5 2026-05-03 21:59:34Z)**_

I agree with your instinct.

The old experience probably feels better because it is **more concrete**. It says:

- Deploy it live
- Make it better
- Add real features

Those are things a non-engineer immediately understands. The new version says:

- The 48-Hour Launch
- The Unstuck System
- Consistent building
- Market validation

That sounds more like a sales framework. It may be useful on a call, but on the page it adds abstraction right where the visitor wants clarity.

I’d change the public-facing module names back toward the old version, but keep the updated 8-week program structure.

My proposed hybrid:

1. **Part 1: Deploy it live**  
   `Week 1`

2. **Part 2: Make it better**  
   `Weeks 2-3`

3. **Part 3: Add real features**  
   `Weeks 4-6`

4. **Part 4: Get real feedback**  
   `Weeks 7-8`

That keeps the old clarity while preserving the new offer.

I’d avoid “The 48-Hour Launch™” on the visible page for now. It sounds catchy, but it also slightly raises suspicion because it feels like a named marketing mechanism. “Deploy it live” is plainer and more believable.

I’d also change “Market validation” to **“Get real feedback”** or **“Put it in front of users.”** Same idea, less consultant-y.

The deeper point: the page should probably use **plain-language outcomes** publicly and save the named frameworks for calls/content.

So yes, safe change I’d make next:

- Replace `Part 1: The 48-Hour Launch™` with `Part 1: Deploy it live`
- Replace `Part 2: The Unstuck System™` with `Part 2: Make it better`
- Replace `Part 3: Consistent building` with `Part 3: Add real features`
- Replace `Part 4: Market validation` with `Part 4: Get real feedback`

This is a good example of the page needing to feel less like “offer architecture” and more like “here’s what we’ll actually do together.”

---

# Context from my IDE setup:

## My request for Codex:
I think I like that. I don't know if I like the weeks underneath, because it implies that everything happens over eight weeks, whereas I think feedback, for example, it's not like you're going to wait until the second month to start showing people your app.


---

_**Agent (gpt-5.5 2026-05-03 22:00:55Z)**_

Yes, I agree. The week labels make the program feel too sequential, like:

1. build first
2. debug later
3. add features later
4. get feedback at the end

That is not how this should feel. Feedback should be part of the whole loop, not a final phase.

I’d remove the week labels and use **activity labels** instead.

For example:

1. **Part 1: Deploy it live**  
   `Launch foundation`

2. **Part 2: Make it better**  
   `Feedback + iteration`

3. **Part 3: Add real features**  
   `Databases, auth, APIs`

4. **Part 4: Get real feedback**  
   `Users + next version`

Or even cleaner:

1. **Part 1: Deploy it live**  
   `Your app online`

2. **Part 2: Make it better**  
   `Debug + iterate`

3. **Part 3: Add real features**  
   `Real product functionality`

4. **Part 4: Get real feedback**  
   `Users + roadmap`

My pick:

- `Your app online`
- `Debug + iterate`
- `Real product functionality`
- `Users + roadmap`

That avoids implying calendar order while still making each part concrete. It also makes the section feel more like “what we’ll work on” instead of “here’s the rigid schedule.”

_**Agent (gpt-5.5 2026-05-03 22:05:31Z)**_

Yes. The current answer is directionally right, but it undersells the difference. It sounds like “video course plus feedback,” when what you mean is closer to **guided implementation**.

I would not say “done-for-you,” though. That could imply you build it for them. This sounds more like **done-with-you** or **guided build program**.

Best version:

> This is not passive content. It’s a guided build program where we walk you through the full path from idea to deployed app, help you make decisions as you build, and get you unstuck when AI, code, or tools break. You work on your own app, not generic lessons.

Slightly shorter:

> This is not passive content. It’s a guided build program where we walk you from idea to deployed app, help you make decisions along the way, and get you unstuck when AI, code, or tools break. You work on your own app, not generic lessons.

I’d use the shorter one.

It says:
- not just videos
- A to Z
- guided path
- unstuck support
- your own app

Much closer to what you’re trying to communicate.

## My request for Codex:
I think this side could also be improved.

You know, I think of it like:
- Week one: deploy it live
- Week two: make it better
- Week three: learn advanced AI workflows
- Week four: learn product design or product management  I don't know exactly, but you know the gist of what I'm trying to get at.


---

_**Agent (gpt-5.5 2026-05-03 22:06:47Z)**_

Yes, this section can be better. Right now it’s too generic and a little repetitive with the module section below. It says what happens, but not enough of the **curriculum arc**.

I’d avoid making it sound like a rigid “Week 1, Week 2...” schedule on the page, but we can imply progression.

A stronger version could be:

**Deploy it live**  
Get your app out of idea-land and onto a real URL so you have something concrete to build from.

**Make it better**  
Use feedback, errors, and messy AI output to improve the app instead of getting stuck or starting over.

**Learn advanced AI workflows**  
Use Cursor, Claude, GitHub, prompts, context, and debugging patterns the way real AI builders use them.

**Design the product**  
Learn how to think through features, user flows, product decisions, and what actually belongs in the app.

That feels more like “A to Z” without overpromising.

For the FAQ answer you mentioned, I’d change it to:

> This is not passive content. It’s a guided build program where we walk you through the full path from idea to deployed app, help you make product and technical decisions, and get you unstuck when AI, code, or tools break. You work on your own app, not generic lessons.

For the section in the screenshot, my recommended four cards are:

1. **Deploy it live**  
   Get your app out of idea-land and onto a real URL so you have something concrete to build from.

2. **Make it better**  
   Turn feedback, errors, and messy AI output into better prompts, safer iterations, and working fixes.

3. **Learn advanced AI workflows**  
   Build with Cursor, Claude, GitHub, prompts, context, and debugging patterns you can reuse on future ideas.

4. **Design the product**  
   Think through user flows, feature decisions, and what actually belongs in the app so you’re building a product, not just screens.

This is stronger because it makes the program feel broader than “deploy/debug/features/feedback” while still staying concrete.

_**Agent (gpt-5.5 2026-05-03 22:14:14Z)**_

Overall, for the actual job of this page — **warm-lead credibility after someone met you in person** — I’d rate it **8/10**.

It is much better than where it started. It now feels like a real 8-week program, not a repurposed workshop page. The biggest remaining weakness is that the page still has a little fuzziness around the exact transformation and some duplicated “experience” language. It is credible, but not yet maximally sharp.

**Alex Hormozi: 7.5/10**
He’d like the concrete value stack: 8 weeks, weekly calls, M-F unstuck support, lifetime access, your own app, schedule a call. He’d probably say the offer is much clearer now, but he’d push for a more tangible “what do I walk away with?” stack.

He’d probably want a box like:

> In 8 weeks, you get:
> Live app, AI build workflow, prompt/debugging system, product design guidance, lifetime content access, weekly calls, M-F support.

He would also question whether removing price helps or hurts. For warm leads, no price is defensible, but he’d want the call process to qualify hard.

**April Dunford: 7/10**
She’d say positioning is improved but still not fully category-sharp. Is this:
- AI app-building program?
- non-technical founder bootcamp?
- guided product build program?
- AI co-founder method?

She’d probably say the ICP is clearer — non-engineers with app ideas — but the category should be made more explicit near the top. The phrase “Vibecoding Course” may still make it feel lighter than the actual program.

Her likely note:

> Stop making people infer the category. Say exactly what this is.

**Donald Miller / StoryBrand: 8.5/10**
He’d like the clarity of:
- “You have an app idea”
- “Now let’s take it live”
- “without waiting on an engineer”
- “Schedule a call”

He’d probably say the hero is understandable within 10 seconds. His main critique would be that the page still talks a lot about the program and not quite enough about the visitor’s pain in plain language.

He might want more lines like:

> Your idea feels too big. You don’t know where to start. We help you break it down and build it.

**Joanna Wiebe / Copyhackers: 7.5/10**
She’d like that the copy is more specific, but she’d want more voice-of-customer language. Right now some lines are good, but still written from the business’s perspective.

She’d probably ask for more phrases like:
- “I have an idea, but I don’t know where to start”
- “I feel like I need an engineer”
- “I tried AI tools and got stuck”
- “I don’t know if what AI gave me is right”

Her critique:

> Good offer copy. Needs more prospect language.

**Seth Godin: 8/10**
He’d probably like that the page is honest and not overpromising paid users or revenue. It speaks to a real worldview: people with ideas no longer need to wait for permission from engineers.

His critique would be around trust and specificity. He’d likely want the page to feel more personally authored by you. Right now it’s professional, but a little anonymous.

He might ask:

> Why you? Why this point of view? Why should this person trust your taste?

**Rob Fitzpatrick / The Mom Test: 7/10**
He’d be skeptical of broad claims like “real apps,” “first users,” “traction,” and “building software with AI” unless backed by concrete examples. He’d like that you removed revenue promises, but he’d still ask whether the page validates demand or assumes it.

His note:

> Show evidence. What have past people built? What happened when they showed it to users?

He’d want less abstract proof and more specific case-study snippets.

**Lenny Rachitsky-style product lens: 8/10**
He’d probably say the ICP and activation moment are clearer now. The activation moment is basically:

> User gets their own app live and realizes they can keep improving it.

That’s strong. He’d likely want the page to clarify success milestones:
- Week 1: app live
- During program: weekly improvements
- End: repeatable workflow + app in motion

His critique:

> The success path is there, but it could be visualized more cleanly.

**My Rating**
For warm leads: **8/10**  
For cold traffic: **6.5/10**  
For high-ticket call booking: **7.5/10**

The fastest path to 9/10:
1. Add a clearer “what you walk away with” list.
2. Add one or two specific case-study/result snippets.
3. Make the category sharper: “guided AI app-building program for non-engineers.”
4. Make the CTA actually schedule a call, not just collect email.
5. Add a short “not for” FAQ to prevent corporate/team-training confusion.

```
# Warm Lead Positioning Notes

These notes capture the current intent for the landing page. The page is not meant to cold-sell strangers. It is meant to support an in-person conversation when someone asks to see a website.

## Primary Visitor

The most likely visitor is a warm lead who met Marko in person and asked for a website afterward.

They are usually a non-technical person with ideas for apps or software they want to build, but they do not know how to turn those ideas into something real.

Examples:

- Working professionals
- College students
- Recruiters
- Marketers
- Nurses
- Other domain experts with app ideas

They are not necessarily startup CEOs or engineering leaders. They are more likely people with domain knowledge, curiosity, and ideas, but without the technical confidence to build.

## Current Situation

They have ideas for apps, automations, or software products, but they feel blocked.

Common beliefs and fears:

- Their idea feels too big.
- They feel like they need an engineer.
- They feel overwhelmed by the tools.
- They do not know where to start.
- They do not know how to break the idea down into something buildable.
- They may have a business idea, but they do not know how to turn it into software.

## Program Container

The program is a two-month / 8-week program.

Earlier docs may say 90 days, but the current direction is 8 weeks. The program changed when the "30 apps in 30 days" concept was replaced with building one app per week.

The current program should be described as an 8-week build cycle, not a one-day workshop, a three-session sprint, or a 90-day program.

There are no cohorts. Avoid "next cohort" language.

## Weekly Build Rhythm

The program is focused on weekly building across 8 weeks.

The participant still has one core app they are working toward. The weekly rhythm should not create the impression that the program is mainly about building a different app every week.

Each week includes one 24-hour sprint. The point is to drill the building muscle, practice the workflow, and make building feel repeatable while still keeping the participant's core app as the main focus.

This should probably be explained more clearly on a call than emphasized heavily before the call.

## Price

The current price is $7,500.

However, the page may not need to show the price. Since this is a high-ticket program and the page is for warm leads, showing the price too early may create unnecessary friction before the value is fully understood.

The page should likely move away from direct checkout language and toward a call-based next step.

## CTA Direction

The goal of the page is to schedule a call.

Possible next steps:

- Book a call
- Fill out an application form
- Pick a date for a conversation

Direct checkout may not be ideal for a $7,500 warm-lead offer. The page should probably support the sales conversation rather than try to close the sale by itself.

Use "Schedule a call" as the primary CTA.

## Outcome

The outcome is not "your app with paid users."

Paid users are not part of the guarantee and should not be implied.

The real outcome is closer to:

- Build and launch real apps with AI.
- Put software in front of real users.
- Learn the repeatable skill of building apps independently.
- Become someone who can turn ideas into working software without waiting on an engineer.
- Walk away with cutting-edge AI app-building workflows.
- Learn prompt engineering, app design, and enough software development lifecycle thinking to build more confidently.

The page should emphasize skill acquisition and repeated building, not a revenue guarantee.

## Promise Boundaries

The page should not promise:

- Paid users
- Revenue
- A guaranteed business outcome
- That the program replaces all engineering needs forever
- That every participant will launch a polished company-ready product

The page can promise:

- A structured 8-week build process
- A repeatable AI app-building workflow
- Support getting unstuck
- Building and shipping weekly
- Increased technical confidence
- Real software built around the participant's own ideas
- Lifetime access to the program content
- Weekly calls
- Support between calls so participants do not stay stuck for days
- Work on the participant's own idea, not generic course projects

## Who This Is Not For

This is not for CEOs buying training for their engineering teams.

This is not primarily for businesses trying to automate internal sales, operations, or admin workflows.

This is not an enterprise AI enablement program.

This is not a general business automation consulting offer.

## Who This Is For

This is for non-engineers who want to build apps, automations, or software that could become businesses.

They may be working professionals, students, or domain experts. The common thread is that they have ideas and want to become capable of building them withou
... (truncated)
```
